([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈtuːlɒ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/tuːˈloʊn, -ˈlɔːn, -ˈlɒn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) French: [[tulɔ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Jules78120-Toulon.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jules78120-Toulon.wav"); [Provençal](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"): *Tolon* (classical norm), *Touloun* (Mistralian norm), pronounced [[tuˈlun]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan")) is a city in the [Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur](/wiki/Provence-Alpes-C%C3%B4te_d%27Azur "Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur") region of southeastern [France](/wiki/France "France"). Located on the [French Riviera](/wiki/French_Riviera "French Riviera") and the historical [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence"), it is the prefecture of the [Var](/wiki/Var_(department) "Var (department)") department.

**Toulon** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ˈtuːlɒ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/tuːˈloʊn, -ˈlɔːn, -ˈlɒn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) French: [[tulɔ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Jules78120-Toulon.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jules78120-Toulon.wav"); [Provençal](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"): *Tolon* (classical norm), *Touloun* (Mistralian norm), pronounced [[tuˈlun]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan")) is a city in the [Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur](/wiki/Provence-Alpes-C%C3%B4te_d%27Azur "Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur") region of southeastern [France](/wiki/France "France"). Located on the [French Riviera](/wiki/French_Riviera "French Riviera") and the historical [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence"), it is the prefecture of the [Var](/wiki/Var_(department) "Var (department)") department.

The Commune of Toulon has a population of 176,198 people (2018), making it France's 13th-largest city, and third most populous French city on the Mediterranean, after [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille") and [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice"). It is the centre of an [urban unit](/wiki/Urban_unit "Urban unit") with 580,281 inhabitants (2018), the ninth largest in France by population.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Toulon is an important centre for naval construction, fishing, wine making, and the manufacture of aeronautical equipment, armaments, maps, paper, tobacco, printing, shoes, and electronic equipment.

The [military port of Toulon](/wiki/Military_port_of_Toulon "Military port of Toulon") is the major [naval](/wiki/Navy "Navy") centre on France's Mediterranean coast, home of the French aircraft carrier *[Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/French_aircraft_carrier_Charles_de_Gaulle "French aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle")* and her battle group. The [French Mediterranean Fleet](/wiki/French_Mediterranean_Fleet "French Mediterranean Fleet") is based in Toulon. The [1793 siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Toulon_(1793) "Siege of Toulon (1793)") took place during the Federalist revolts.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Timeline of Toulon](/wiki/Timeline_of_Toulon "Timeline of Toulon")

### Prehistory to the Roman era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistory to the Roman era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Cath%C3%A9drale_Notre-Dame-de-la-Seds_de_Toulon-7976.jpg/250px-Cath%C3%A9drale_Notre-Dame-de-la-Seds_de_Toulon-7976.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cath%C3%A9drale_Notre-Dame-de-la-Seds_de_Toulon-7976.jpg)

Toulon Cathedral (11th to 18th centuries)

Archaeological excavations, such as those at the [Cosquer Cave](/wiki/Cosquer_Cave "Cosquer Cave") near [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille"), show that the coast of [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence") was inhabited since at least the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") era. [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") colonists came from [Phocaea](/wiki/Phocaea "Phocaea"), [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor"), in about the 7th century BC and established trading depots along the coast, including one, called Olbia, at Saint-Pierre de l'Almanarre south of [Hyères](/wiki/Hy%C3%A8res "Hyères"), to the east of Toulon. The [Ligurians](/wiki/Ligures "Ligures") settled in the area beginning in the 4th century BC.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In the 2nd century BC, the residents of Massalia (present-day [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille")) called upon the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Romans "Ancient Romans") to help them [pacify](/wiki/Pacification_(military_action) "Pacification (military action)") the region. The Romans defeated the Ligurians and began to start their own colonies along the coast.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A Roman settlement was founded at the present location of Toulon, with the name Telo Martius – Telo, either for the local god of springs [Telo](/wiki/Telo_(mythology) "Telo (mythology)") or from the Latin *tol*, the base of the hill – and [Martius](/wiki/Mars_(mythology) "Mars (mythology)"), for the god of war. Telo Martius became one of the two principal Roman [dye](/wiki/Dye "Dye") manufacturing centres, producing the purple colour used in imperial robes, made from the local sea snail called [murex](/wiki/Murex "Murex"), and from the acorns of the oak trees. Toulon harbour became a shelter for trading ships, and the name of the town gradually changed from Telo to Tholon, Tolon, and Toulon.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Arrival of Christianity and the Counts of Provence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Arrival of Christianity and the Counts of Provence")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=). *(July 2019)* |

Toulon was Christianized in the 5th century, and the first cathedral built. [Honoratus](/wiki/Honoratus "Honoratus") and Gratianus of Toulon (Gratien), according to the *[Gallia Christiana](/wiki/Gallia_Christiana "Gallia Christiana")*, were the first bishops of [Toulon](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Toulon "Bishopric of Toulon"), but [Louis Duchesne](/wiki/Louis_Duchesne "Louis Duchesne") gives [Augustalis](/wiki/Augustalis_(bishop) "Augustalis (bishop)") as the first historical bishop. He assisted at councils in 441 and 442 and signed in 449 and 450 the letters addressed to [Pope Leo I](/wiki/Pope_Leo_I "Pope Leo I") from the [province of Arles](/wiki/Province_of_Arles "Province of Arles").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A [Saint Cyprian](/wiki/Cyprian_of_Toulon "Cyprian of Toulon"), disciple and biographer of [St. Cæsarius of Arles](/wiki/Caesarius_of_Arles "Caesarius of Arles"), is also mentioned as a Bishop of Toulon. His episcopate, begun in 524, had not come to an end in 541; he converted to Catholicism two [Visigothic](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") chiefs, Mandrier and Flavian, who became [anchorites](/wiki/Anchorite "Anchorite") and martyrs on the peninsula of Mandrier.[[9]](#cite_note-9) As barbarians invaded the region and Roman power crumbled, the town was frequently attacked by pirates and the [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens "Saracens").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1486, Provence became part of France. Soon afterwards, in 1494, [Charles VIII of France](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_France "Charles VIII of France"), with the intention of making France a sea power on the Mediterranean, and to support his military campaign in Italy, began constructing a military port at the harbor of Toulon. His Italian campaign failed, and in 1497, the rulers of [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), who controlled commerce on that part of the Mediterranean, blockaded the new port.

In 1524, as part of his longtime battle against Emperor [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") and the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), King [François I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") of [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") completed a powerful new fort, the [Tour Royale, Toulon](/wiki/Tour_Royale,_Toulon "Tour Royale, Toulon"), at the entrance of the harbour. However, a few months later the commander of the new fort sold it to the commander of an Army of the Holy Roman Empire, and Toulon surrendered.

In 1543, Francis I found a surprising new ally in his battle against the Holy Roman Empire. He invited the fleet of [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") Admiral [Barbarossa](/wiki/Hayreddin_Barbarossa "Hayreddin Barbarossa") to Toulon as part of the [Franco-Ottoman alliance](/wiki/Franco-Ottoman_alliance "Franco-Ottoman alliance"). The residents were forced to leave (except for the heads of household), and the Ottoman sailors occupied the town for the winter.[[11]](#cite_note-11) See [Ottoman occupation of Toulon](/wiki/Ottoman_occupation_of_Toulon "Ottoman occupation of Toulon").

In 1646, a fleet was gathered in Toulon for the major [Battle of Orbetello](/wiki/Battle_of_Orbetello "Battle of Orbetello"), also known as the Battle of Isola del Giglio, commanded by France's first Grand Admiral, the young Grand Admiral Marquis of Brézé, Jean Armand de Maillé-Bréze of 36 galleons, 20 galleys, and a large complement of minor vessels. This fleet carried aboard an army of 8,000 infantry and 800 cavalry and its baggage under Thomas of Savoy, shortly before a general in Spanish service.

King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") was determined to make France a major sea power. In 1660, his Minister [Jean-Baptiste Colbert](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert "Jean-Baptiste Colbert") ordered [Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Le_Prestre_de_Vauban "Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban") to build a new arsenal and to fortify the town. In 1707, during the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"), Toulon successfully resisted a siege by the Imperial Army led by Duke [Victor Amadeus II of Sardinia](/wiki/Victor_Amadeus_II_of_Sardinia "Victor Amadeus II of Sardinia") of [Savoy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Savoy "Duchy of Savoy") and [Prince Eugene](/wiki/Prince_Eugene_of_Savoy "Prince Eugene of Savoy"). However, in 1720, the city was ravaged by the [black plague](/wiki/Black_plague "Black plague"), coming from Marseille. Thirteen thousand people, or half the population, died.

In 1790, following the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), Toulon became the administrative centre of the département of the [Var](/wiki/Var_(department) "Var (department)"). However, in 1793, the [Jacobin](/wiki/Jacobin "Jacobin") administration of the city was swept from power, allowing [Girondins](/wiki/Girondins "Girondins") and royalists to take their place; the city then rose up against the central administration of the First Republic and joined the [Federalist revolts](/wiki/Federalist_revolts "Federalist revolts"). The new Federalist administration surrendered the city and its fleet to the British. French Republican forces then undertook the [siege of Toulon](/wiki/Siege_of_Toulon "Siege of Toulon"), forcing the British to withdraw, taking a number of ships with them and destroying the rest of them. [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") served as an artillery captain during the event. To punish Toulon for its rebellion, the town lost its status as department capital and was briefly renamed ***Port-la-Montagne***,[[12]](#cite_note-Archives-12) after [The Mountain](/wiki/The_Mountain "The Mountain") faction.

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Toulon_1850.jpg/250px-Toulon_1850.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toulon_1850.jpg)

View in 1850

During the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), from 1803 until 1805 a British fleet led by Admiral [Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson "Horatio Nelson") blockaded Toulon.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 1820, the statue which became known as the [Venus de Milo](/wiki/Venus_de_Milo "Venus de Milo") was discovered on the Greek island of Milo and seen by a French naval officer, Emile Voutier. He persuaded the French Ambassador to Turkey to buy it, and brought it to Toulon on his ship, the *Estafette*. From Toulon it was taken to the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1849, Louis-Napoleon named [Georges Eugène Haussmann](/wiki/Georges_Eug%C3%A8ne_Haussmann "Georges Eugène Haussmann") as the new Prefect of the [Var](/wiki/Var_(department) "Var (department)") department. He served there only one year, but he laid out the current street plan for the city center, as he would later do for the city of Paris.

In the 1890s, there were the [Kronstadt-Toulon naval visits](/wiki/Kronstadt%E2%80%93Toulon_naval_visits "Kronstadt–Toulon naval visits").

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/H%C3%B4tel_Ville_Toulon_1.jpg/250px-H%C3%B4tel_Ville_Toulon_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:H%C3%B4tel_Ville_Toulon_1.jpg)

[Hôtel de Ville](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Toulon "Hôtel de Ville, Toulon")

In August 1935, a year before the reign of the [Popular Front](/wiki/Popular_Front_(France) "Popular Front (France)"), violent uprisings of the workers of the Toulon shipyards opposed the [policy of austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity"). This resulted in a large number of deaths and injuries; a [state of emergency](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency") was imposed.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

During World War II, after the [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") landings in North Africa ([Operation Torch](/wiki/Operation_Torch "Operation Torch")) the German Army occupied southern France ([Case Anton](/wiki/Case_Anton "Case Anton")), leading French naval officers to [scuttle the French Fleet based at Toulon](/wiki/Scuttling_of_the_French_fleet_in_Toulon "Scuttling of the French fleet in Toulon") on 27 November 1942.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The city was bombed by the Allies in November of the following year, with much of the port destroyed and five hundred residents killed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Hôtel de Ville](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Toulon "Hôtel de Ville, Toulon"), centre of the administration of the city, was completed in 1970.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 1979, the [University of Toulon](/wiki/University_of_the_South,_Toulon-Var "University of the South, Toulon-Var") opened. Toulon was one of four French cities where the extreme-right [Front National](/wiki/Front_National_(France) "Front National (France)") won the local elections in 1995. The Front National was voted out of power in 2001.

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Main sights")]

### Old Town

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Old Town")]

The old town of Toulon, the historic centre between the port, the Boulevard de Strasbourg and the Cours Lafayette, is a pedestrian area with narrow streets, small squares and many fountains. [Toulon Cathedral](/wiki/Toulon_Cathedral "Toulon Cathedral") is there. The area is also home of the celebrated [Provençal market](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_markets "Provençal markets"), which takes place every morning on the Cours Lafayette and features local products. The old town decayed in the 1980s and 1990s, but recently many of the fountains and squares have been restored and many new shops have opened.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

  View of downtown Toulon and Mediterranean Sea from Mount Faron

The Old Town of Toulon is known for its fountains, found in many of the small squares, each with a different character. The original system of fountains was built in the late 17th century; most were rebuilt in the 18th or early 19th century and have recently been restored.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Upper Town of Georges-Eugène Haussmann

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Upper Town of Georges-Eugène Haussmann")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Toulon_place_de_la_libert%C3%A9-fontaine.jpg/250px-Toulon_place_de_la_libert%C3%A9-fontaine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toulon_place_de_la_libert%C3%A9-fontaine.jpg)

Place de la Liberté.

The upper town, between the Boulevard de Strasbourg and the railway station, was built in the mid-19th century under [Louis Napoleon](/wiki/Louis_Napoleon "Louis Napoleon"). The project was begun by [Georges-Eugène Haussmann](/wiki/Georges-Eug%C3%A8ne_Haussmann "Georges-Eugène Haussmann"), who was prefect of the Var in 1849. Improvements to the neighbourhood included the [Toulon Opera](/wiki/Toulon_Opera "Toulon Opera"), the Place de la Liberté, the Grand Hôtel, the Gardens of Alexander I, the Chalucet Hospital, the Palais de Justice, the train station, and the building now occupied by [Galeries Lafayette](/wiki/Galeries_Lafayette "Galeries Lafayette"), among others. Haussmann went on to use the same style on a much grander scale in the rebuilding of central Paris.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Harbour and Arsenal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Harbour and Arsenal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Toulon_Rade_and_Arsenal.jpg/250px-Toulon_Rade_and_Arsenal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toulon_Rade_and_Arsenal.jpg)

View of Toulon, the Arsenal and Mount Faron from the Harbour.

Main article: [Military port of Toulon](/wiki/Military_port_of_Toulon "Military port of Toulon")

Toulon harbour is one of the best natural anchorages on the Mediterranean and one of the largest harbours in Europe. A naval arsenal and shipyard was built in 1599, and a small sheltered harbour, the Veille Darse, was built in 1604–1610 to protect ships from the wind and sea. The shipyard was greatly enlarged by [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu"), who wished to make France a Mediterranean naval power. Further additions were made by [Jean-Baptiste Colbert](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert "Jean-Baptiste Colbert") and [Vauban](/wiki/Vauban "Vauban").

### Le Mourillon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Le Mourillon")]

Main article: [Le Mourillon](/wiki/Le_Mourillon "Le Mourillon")

Le Mourillon is a small seaside neighbourhood to the east of Toulon, near the entrance of the harbour. It was once a fishing village, and then became the home of many of the officers of the French fleet. Mourillon has a small fishing port, next to a 16th-century fort, Fort Saint Louis, which was reconstructed by Vauban.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the 1970s, the city of Toulon built a series of sheltered sandy beaches in Mourillon, which today are very popular with the Toulonais and naval families. The Museum of Asian Art is in a house on the waterfront near Fort St. Louis.

### Mount Faron

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Mount Faron")]

Main article: [Mont Faron](/wiki/Mont_Faron "Mont Faron")

Mount Faron (584 metres (1,916 feet)) dominates the city of Toulon. The top can be reached by cable car from Toulon or by a narrow road that ascends from the west side and descends on the east side. The road is one of the stages of the annual [Paris–Nice](/wiki/Paris%E2%80%93Nice "Paris–Nice") and [Tour Méditerranéen](/wiki/Tour_M%C3%A9diterran%C3%A9en "Tour Méditerranéen") bicycle races.

At the top of Mount Faron is a memorial dedicated to the 1944 Allied landings in Provence ([Operation Dragoon](/wiki/Operation_Dragoon "Operation Dragoon")), and to the liberation of Toulon.

### Vauban's fortifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Vauban's fortifications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Wikipedia-porte-italie.jpg/500px-Wikipedia-porte-italie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wikipedia-porte-italie.jpg)

The Porte d'Italie, built by Vauban. Napoleon departed from this gate in 1796 on his Italian campaign.

Beginning in 1678, [Vauban](/wiki/Vauban "Vauban") constructed an elaborate system of fortifications around Toulon. Some parts, such as the section that once ran along the present-day Boulevard de Strasbourg, were removed in the mid-19th century so the city could be enlarged, but other parts remain.[[22]](#cite_note-22) One part that can be visited is the Porte d'Italie, one of the old city gates. [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") departed on his triumphant Italian campaign from this gate in 1796.

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Museums")]

Toulon has a number of museums.

The **Museum of the French Navy** (Musée national de la marine) is located on Place Monsenergue, next on the west side of the old port, a short distance from the Hôtel de Ville. The museum was founded in 1814, during the reign of the Emperor Napoleon.[[23]](#cite_note-23) It is located today behind what was formerly the monumental gate to the Arsenal of Toulon, built in 1738. The museum building, along with the clock tower next to it, is one of the few buildings of the port and arsenal which survived Allied bombardments during World War II. It contains displays tracing the history of Toulon as a port of the [French Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy"). Highlights include large 18th-century ship models used to teach seamanship and models of the aircraft carrier *[Charles De Gaulle](/wiki/French_aircraft_carrier_Charles_de_Gaulle_(R91) "French aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle (R91)")*.

The **Museum of Old Toulon and its Region** (Musée du vieux Toulon et de sa région). The Museum was founded in 1912, and contains a collection of maps, paintings, drawings, models and other artifacts showing the history of the city.

The **Museum of Asian Arts** (Musée des arts asiatiques), in Mourillon. Located in a house with garden which once belonged to the son and later the grandson of author [Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne"), the museum contains a small but interesting collection of art objects, many donated by naval officers from the time of the French colonization of Southeast Asia. It includes objects and paintings from India, China, Southeast Asia, China Tibet and Japan.

The **Museum of Art** (Musée d'art) was created in 1888, and contains collections of modern and contemporary art, as well as paintings of Provence from the 17th century to the beginning of the 20th century. It owns works by landscape artists of Provence from the late 19th century ([Paul Guigou](/wiki/Paul_Guigou "Paul Guigou"), [Auguste Aiguier](/wiki/Auguste_Aiguier "Auguste Aiguier"), [Vincent Courdouan](/wiki/Vincent_Courdouan "Vincent Courdouan"), [Félix Ziem](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Ziem "Félix Ziem")), and the Fauves of Provence ([Charles Camoin](/wiki/Charles_Camoin "Charles Camoin"), [Auguste Chabaud](/wiki/Auguste_Chabaud "Auguste Chabaud"), [Louis Mathieu Verdilhan](/wiki/Louis_Mathieu_Verdilhan "Louis Mathieu Verdilhan")). The contemporary collections contain works from 1960 to today representing the New Realism Movement (Arman, César, [Christo](/wiki/Christo "Christo"), Klein, Raysse); Minimalist Art ([Sol LeWitt](/wiki/Sol_LeWitt "Sol LeWitt"), [Donald Judd](/wiki/Donald_Judd "Donald Judd")); Support Surface (Cane, Viallat côtoient Arnal, Buren, Chacallis) and an important collection of photographs by [Henri Cartier-Bresson](/wiki/Henri_Cartier-Bresson "Henri Cartier-Bresson"), [Dieuzaide](/w/index.php?title=Dieuzaide&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dieuzaide (page does not exist)"), [Edouard Boubat](/wiki/Edouard_Boubat "Edouard Boubat"), [Willy Ronis](/wiki/Willy_Ronis "Willy Ronis") and [André Kertész](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Kert%C3%A9sz "André Kertész")).[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The **Memorial Museum to the Landings in Provence** (Mémorial du débarquement de Provence) is located on the summit of Mount Faron, this small museum, opened in 1964 by President [Charles De Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_De_Gaulle "Charles De Gaulle"), commemorates the Allied landing in Provence in August 1944 with photos, weapons and models.

The **Museum of Natural History of Toulon and the Var** (Musée d'histoire naturelle de Toulon et du Var) was founded in 1888, has a large collection of displays about dinosaurs, birds, mammals, and minerals, mostly from the region.

The **Hôtel des arts** was opened in 1998, presents five exhibits a year of works by well-known contemporary artists. Featured artists have included [Sean Scully](/wiki/Sean_Scully "Sean Scully"), [Jannis Kounellis](/wiki/Jannis_Kounellis "Jannis Kounellis"), [Claude Viallat](/wiki/Claude_Viallat "Claude Viallat"), [Per Kirkeby](/wiki/Per_Kirkeby "Per Kirkeby"), and [Vik Muniz](/wiki/Vik_Muniz "Vik Muniz").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Toulon_Rade_Sunset.jpg/250px-Toulon_Rade_Sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toulon_Rade_Sunset.jpg)

The Harbour at Sunset

Toulon is subtropical, featuring a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): Csa), characterised by abundant and strong sunshine, dry summers, and rain which is rare but sometimes torrential; and by hot summers and mild winters. Because of its proximity to the sea, the temperature is relatively moderate.

The average temperature in January, the coldest month, is 9.9 °C (50 °F), the warmest of any city in metropolitan France.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In January, the maximum average temperature is 13.2 °C (56 °F). and the average minimum temperature is 6.6 °C (44 °F).

The average temperature in July, the warmest month, is 24.7 °C (76 °F), with an average maximum of 29.5 °C (85 °F). and an average minimal temperature of 19.9 °C (68 °F).

According to data collected by [Météo-France](/wiki/M%C3%A9t%C3%A9o-France "Météo-France"), Toulon is the second city (after [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille")) in metropolitan France with the most sunshine per year: an average of 2,854.1 hours a year from 1991 to 2020, compared with 2,695 hours a year for Nice and 2,472 hours for Perpignan.[[26]](#cite_note-26) This is due to the wall of mountains that largely protects Toulon from the weather coming from the north. With a yearly average temperature of 16.7 °C (62 °F), it is also one of the warmest cities in metropolitan France.

One distinctive feature of the Toulon climate is the wind, with 115 days a year of strong winds; usually either the cold and dry [Mistral](/wiki/Mistral_(wind) "Mistral (wind)") or the [Tramontane](/wiki/Tramontane "Tramontane") from the north, the wet [Marin](/wiki/Marin_(wind) "Marin (wind)"); or the [Sirocco](/wiki/Sirocco "Sirocco") sometimes bearing reddish sand from Africa; or the wet and stormy [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") from the east.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] (See [Winds of Provence](/wiki/Winds_of_Provence "Winds of Provence").) The windiest month is January, with an average of 12.5 days of strong winds. The least windy month is September, with 7 days of strong winds.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Education")]

Toulon has a [conservatory](/wiki/Music_school "Music school") ([Conservatoire TPM](/w/index.php?title=Conservatoire_TPM&action=edit&redlink=1 "Conservatoire TPM (page does not exist)"), part of [Conservatoire à rayonnement régional de Toulon](/w/index.php?title=Conservatoire_%C3%A0_rayonnement_r%C3%A9gional_de_Toulon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Conservatoire à rayonnement régional de Toulon (page does not exist)")) which teaches music, theater, dance and circus and an [art academy](/wiki/Art_school "Art school") called [École supérieure d'art et de design Toulon Provence Méditerranée](/w/index.php?title=%C3%89cole_sup%C3%A9rieure_d%27art_et_de_design_Toulon_Provence_M%C3%A9diterran%C3%A9e&action=edit&redlink=1 "École supérieure d'art et de design Toulon Provence Méditerranée (page does not exist)"). Toulon is also home to a number of institutes of the [University of Toulon](/wiki/University_of_the_South,_Toulon-Var "University of the South, Toulon-Var"), known until 2013 as University of the South, Toulon-Var.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Toulon has a campus of [KEDGE Business School](/wiki/KEDGE_Business_School "KEDGE Business School").

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Literature")]

Toulon figures prominently in [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo")'s *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables "Les Misérables")*. It is the location of the infamous prison, the [bagne of Toulon](/wiki/Bagne_of_Toulon "Bagne of Toulon"), in which the protagonist Jean Valjean spends nineteen years in hard labour. Toulon is also where Valjean first encounters the novel's antagonist, [Javert](/wiki/Javert "Javert"). One portion of the wall of the old bagne, or prison, where Jean Valjean was supposedly held still stands to the right of the entrance of the Old Harbour.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In [Anthony Powell](/wiki/Anthony_Powell "Anthony Powell")'s novel *[What's Become of Waring](/wiki/What%27s_Become_of_Waring "What's Become of Waring")* the central characters spend a long summer holiday in Toulon's old town. Powell himself stayed at the Hotel du Port et des Negociants on two occasions in the early 1930s and writes in the second volume of his memoirs *The naval port, with its small inner harbour, row of cafés along the rade, was quite separate from the business quarter of the town. A paddle steamer plied several times a day between this roadstead and the agreeably unsophisticated plage of Les Sablettes*.

[Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad")'s last novel, *[The Rover](/wiki/The_Rover_(novel) "The Rover (novel)")*, is also set around Toulon.

The last half of [Dewey Lambdin](/wiki/Dewey_Lambdin "Dewey Lambdin")'s historical fiction novel, *H.M.S. Cockerel*, (the sixth novel in his [Alan Lewrie](/wiki/Alan_Lewrie "Alan Lewrie") naval adventure series) details the [Siege of Toulon](/wiki/Siege_of_Toulon "Siege of Toulon") from Lewrie's perspective, as he commands a commandeered French barge carrying sea mortars against Lieutenant-Colonel Bonaparte's forces.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/2016_Toulon_-_Hafen.jpg/250px-2016_Toulon_-_Hafen.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_Toulon_-_Hafen.jpg)

Harbor with ferry

Toulon is served by the [Gare de Toulon](/wiki/Gare_de_Toulon "Gare de Toulon") railway station, offering suburban services to Marseille (1 train every 15 minutes during peak hours), regional services to Nice, and high speed connections to Paris, Strasburg, Luxemburg and Brussels. A daily night intercity train is also connecting the city to Nice, Marseille and Paris.

The port of Toulon is the main port of departures for ferries to [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica").

The nearest airport is the regional [Toulon-Hyères Airport](/wiki/Toulon-Hy%C3%A8res_Airport "Toulon-Hyères Airport"). The proximity of [Marseille-Provence Airport](/wiki/Marseille-Provence_Airport "Marseille-Provence Airport") located at 80 kms of the city, serving international destinations in Europe, Africa, Middle East, North America and Asia and linked to the city-center by direct trains daily offers a good international connectivity to the city.

The [A50 autoroute](/wiki/A50_autoroute "A50 autoroute") connects Toulon to Marseille, the [A57 autoroute](/wiki/A57_autoroute "A57 autoroute") runs from Toulon to Le Luc, where it connects to the [A8 autoroute](/wiki/A8_autoroute "A8 autoroute").

The local public transport service, [Réseau Mistral de Toulon](/w/index.php?title=R%C3%A9seau_Mistral_de_Toulon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Réseau Mistral de Toulon (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_Mistral_de_Toulon "fr:Réseau Mistral de Toulon")], operates 60 bus routes and 3 sea shuttle lines and is used by 30 million passengers annually.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Gastronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Gastronomy")]

Local food highlights include:

* cuisine from the Mediterranean and from Provence
* the *cade toulonnaise*, a local speciality composed of chickpea flour and which is equivalent to the [Socca](/wiki/Farinata "Farinata") of Nice
* the Chichi Frégi, a type of donut from Provence.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toulon&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sport")]

The most successful of the city's clubs are the [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") team [RC Toulon](/wiki/RC_Toulonnais "RC Toulonnais") and the women's handball team [Toulon St-Cyr Var Handball](/wiki/Toulon_St-Cyr_Var_Handball "Toulon St-Cyr Var Handball"), both playing in the top division of their respective sports. The basketball team [Hyères-Toulon Var Basket](/wiki/Hy%C3%A8res-Toulon_Var_Basket "Hyères-Toulon Var Basket") play in the second division of the [French championship](/wiki/Ligue_Nationale_de_Basket_(LNB) "Ligue Nationale de Basket (LNB)").

The city hosts the final four of the annual [Toulon Tournament](/wiki/Toulon_Tournament "Toulon Tournament"), an international under-21 [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") tournament.

Toulon's main football team is [Sporting Club Toulon](/wiki/Sporting_Toulon_Var "Sporting Toulon Var"), which plays in [Championnat National](/wiki/Championnat_National "Championnat National"), the third level of French football. Famous players such as [Delio Onnis](/wiki/Delio_Onnis "Delio Onnis"), [Jean Tigana](/wiki/Jean_Tigana "Jean Tigana"), [Christian Dalger](/wiki/Christian_Dalger "Christian Dalger"), [David Ginola](/wiki/David_Ginola "David Ginola") and [Sébastien Squillaci](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Squillaci "Sébastien Squillaci") have all played for Sporting.

The city has been chosen by [Groupama](/wiki/Groupama "Groupama") [Team France](https://web.archive.org/web/20170129122424/http://groupamateamfrance.americascup.com/) as the venue for the fifth event in the Americas Cup World Series 2016, alongside international cities such as Portsmouth & New York.
